sections.title: 1. பாடத்தின் முழுமையான சுருக்கம் (Detailed Summary)
sections.content:
Once upon a time, in a village called La Mancha in Spain, lived a man who loved to read about knights. He read about courageous knights and their incredible adventures every day. He was so absorbed in these stories that he hardly remembered to eat or sleep. The exciting adventures and romances of knights and squires came alive in his imagination. As years passed, Don Quixote imagined himself placed in the world of knights. He convinced Sancho Panza, a peasant from the same village, to be his servant (squire) by promising to reward him with an island.

The Windmill Incident: Don Quixote and Sancho Panza set out on their journey. On a hill, they saw a large old windmill. Don Quixote's imagination took over, and he mistook the windmill for a hideous ogre blocking their path. Despite Sancho's warnings that it was just a windmill, Don Quixote charged at it with his horse, Rocinante, at full speed. He collided with the windmill and fell. Even after falling, he refused to accept reality, claiming that the 'ogre' was more seriously wounded than he was.

The Sheep Incident: As they passed a farm, they saw a thick cloud of dust. Don Quixote imagined it to be a prodigious army marching towards them with steeds, trumpets, and drums. Sancho corrected him, saying it was just the bleating of sheep and lambs. Ignoring Sancho's entreaties, Don Quixote charged into the midst of the sheep. The frightened animals fled helter-skelter. The shepherds, seeing this disorder, pelted stones at him until he fell wounded to the ground.

Return to Reality: As Don Quixote passed by, the local peasants thought he was a madman and mocked him. Don Quixote misunderstood their mockery as cheers for his great deeds. However, upon returning to his estate, Don Quixote finally realized that all his adventures were merely a product of his imagination. He never embarrassed himself further by going on any more quests. Instead, his house was always full of children to whom he told fantastic stories about knights, squires, and noble quests, living happily ever after.

sections.title: 2. கூடுதல் பகுதிகள் மற்றும் குறிப்புகள் (Extra Points & Character Sketch)
sections.content:
1. Don Quixote: An idealistic but delusional man who loses touch with reality due to his obsession with chivalric romances. He represents the theme of idealism vs. reality.
2. Sancho Panza: A realistic and practical peasant who serves as a foil to Don Quixote. He represents common sense.
3. Rocinante: Don Quixote's trusty steed (horse).
4. Setting: The story begins in a village called La Mancha in Spain.
5. The Core Theme: To Dream the Impossible Dream. Don Quixote can be seen not simply as crazy, but as a person who wants to accomplish a greater good and refuses to compromise his ideals, much like real-world idealists.

sections.title: 3. நினைவில் கொள்க மற்றும் ஒரு மதிப்பெண் பகுதிகள் (Points to Remember & Glossary)
sections.content:
GLOSSARY:
1. knight - a man who served his lord as a mounted soldier in armour.
2. incredible - unbelievable.
3. hilarious - amusing, entertaining.
4. ogre - monster, giant.
5. combat - fighting between armed forces.
6. prodigious - impressively great in extent.
7. steed - horse that is ridden.
8. spur - a device with a small spike or a spiked wheel that is worn on a rider's heel and used for urging a horse forward.

KEY EVENTS:
- The reward promised to Sancho: An island.
- The illusion of the windmill: Mistaken for a hideous ogre.
- The illusion of the dust cloud: Mistaken for a prodigious army of knights.
- The reality of the dust cloud: A flock of sheep and lambs.
- The reaction of the peasants: Mocked him, calling him a madman.

sections.title: 4. சொல்வளம் (Vocabulary: Homophones, Homonyms, Syllabification)
sections.content:
HOMOPHONES (Words spelled differently but having the same sound):
1. see / sea (I can see the sea.)
2. knight / night (The knight fought at night.)
3. right / write (Write the right answer.)
4. arms / alms (He gave alms with his own arms.)
5. fair / fare (The bus fare to the fair is cheap.)
6. here / hear (Come here to hear the music.)
7. heard / herd (I heard the herd of cattle.)
8. our / hour (Our journey takes one hour.)
9. no / know (No, I don't know him.)
10. not / knot (Do not tie a tight knot.)

HOMONYMS (Spelled the same way but differ in meaning):
1. rose (got up) / rose (a flower)
2. fair (beautiful/just) / fair (an exhibition/event)
3. saw (past tense of see) / saw (a tool for cutting wood)
4. right (correct) / right (direction)
5. well (a water source) / well (in good health)
6. march (to walk in a military manner) / March (the month)
7. can (ability) / can (a metal container)
8. live (to exist) / live (alive, not dead)
9. tears (drops of water from eyes) / tears (rips or pulls apart)
10. second (number two in line) / second (a unit of time)

SYLLABIFICATION (Dividing words into syllables):
1. ad/ven/ture (3 syllables)
2. cou/ra/geous (3 syllables)
3. in/cred/i/ble (4 syllables)
4. knight (1 syllable)
5. hi/lar/i/ous (4 syllables)
6. ex/cite/ment (3 syllables)
7. peas/ant (2 syllables)
8. i/mag/ine (3 syllables)
9. shep/herd (2 syllables)
10. en/treat (2 syllables)

sections.title: 5. இலக்கணம் (Grammar: Degrees of Comparison)
sections.content:
SUPERLATIVE ADJECTIVES (From Crossword):
1. driest (Deserts are the driest places on Earth.)
2. oldest (The oldest man ever whose age has been verified is Jiroemon Kimura.)
3. largest (Russia is the largest country in the world. / The elephant is the largest animal.)
4. closest (Mercury is the closest planet to the sun.)
5. coldest (Antarctica is the coldest continent in the world.)
6. smallest (Vatican City is the smallest country in the world.)
7. hardest (Diamonds are the hardest material in the world.)
8. fastest (The cheetah is the fastest animal in the world.)
9. highest (Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world.)

LANGUAGE CHECK POINT (Rules for Degrees of Comparison):
- Rule 1: When two degrees of adjectives are used, if the first one is superlative, the second one also should be superlative. (Correct: George is the smartest and most intelligent staff of this company. Incorrect: George is the smartest and intelligent staff.)
- Rule 2: When both adjectives are in the same degree, the adjective having 'more' must come after the adjective having 'er'. (Correct: Kayal is smarter and more intelligent than Saral. Incorrect: Kayal is more intelligent and smarter than Saral.)
- Rule 3: If any possessive pronoun or possessive case precedes the superlative degree, then 'the' cannot be used. (Correct: It is my best genre of music. Incorrect: It is my the best genre of music.)

quiz.question: In which country is the village of La Mancha located?
quiz.explanation: The story begins with: 'Once upon a time, in a village called La Mancha, in Spain...'

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote love to read about every day?
options: Courageous knights and their incredible adventures
quiz.explanation: He loved to read about courageous knights and the incredible adventures they had.

quiz.question: What was the physical consequence of Don Quixote's obsession with reading stories of knights?
options: He hardly remembered to eat or sleep.
quiz.explanation: He was so absorbed in these stories that he hardly remembered to eat or sleep.

quiz.question: Who was Sancho Panza before he became Don Quixote's squire?
options: A peasant from the same village
quiz.explanation: Don Quixote convinces Sancho Panza, a peasant from the same village, to be his squire.

quiz.question: What reward did Don Quixote promise Sancho Panza for serving him faithfully?
quiz.explanation: Don Quixote promised: 'Yes, if you serve me faithfully and honestly, I will reward you with an island.'

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote mistake the large old windmill for?
quiz.explanation: When he saw the windmill, he said, 'Look Sancho, how dare that hideous ogre block our path?'

quiz.question: What was the name of Don Quixote's horse?
quiz.explanation: The text mentions, 'Don Quixote and Rocinante charged the windmill at full speed.'

quiz.question: What happened to Don Quixote when he charged the windmill?
options: He flew towards the windmill and collided with it.
The windmill ran away in fear.
quiz.explanation: Don Quixote flew towards the windmill and collided with it, falling to the ground.

quiz.question: How did Don Quixote explain his defeat against the windmill?
options: He claimed the ogre was more seriously wounded than he was.
quiz.explanation: After falling, he said, 'Look Sancho! The ogre is more seriously wounded than I am!'

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote and Sancho see that caused a thick cloud of dust?
options: A flock of sheep and lambs
quiz.explanation: The thick cloud of dust was actually caused by a flock of sheep and lambs, though Don Quixote thought it was an army.

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote imagine the thick cloud of dust to be?
options: A prodigious army marching in their direction
quiz.explanation: Don told him that there was undoubtedly a prodigious army marching in their direction.

quiz.question: What sounds did Don Quixote claim to hear from the imaginary army?
options: The neighing of steeds, the sound of trumpets, and the rattling of drums
The shouting of soldiers and the clashing of swords
The roaring of lions and the beating of drums
quiz.explanation: He said, 'At least you must hear the neighing of steeds, the sound of trumpets, and the rattling of drums.'

quiz.question: What did Sancho Panza hear instead of trumpets and drums?
options: The bleating of many sheep and lambs
quiz.explanation: Sancho said, 'I hear nothing, but the bleating of many sheep and lambs.'

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote do when he reached the flock of sheep?
options: He asked the shepherds for directions.
He set spurs to Rocinante and charged into the midst of the sheep.
He turned back and ran away.
He bought the sheep from the farmers.
quiz.explanation: He charged into the sheep, scattering them helter-skelter.

quiz.question: How did the shepherds react to Don Quixote's attack on their sheep?
options: They ran away leaving the sheep.
They pelted stones at him until he fell wounded.
quiz.explanation: The shepherds pelted stones that soon he fell wounded to the ground.

quiz.question: What did the local peasants call Don Quixote when he passed by?
quiz.explanation: The peasants laughed and said, 'Look, it’s Don Quixote, the madman!'

quiz.question: How did Don Quixote interpret the mocking laughter of the peasants?
options: He thought they were throwing stones.
He thought they were welcoming him and cheering for his great deeds.
He got angry and attacked them.
quiz.explanation: He told Sancho, 'See how the people welcome us! They must have heard of our great deeds!'

quiz.question: What realization did Don Quixote have after returning to his estate?
options: That all his adventures were simply a product of his imagination.
That he needed a better sword.
That the windmills were actually dragons.
quiz.explanation: Returned to his estate, Don Quixote realized that all his adventures were simply a product of his imagination.

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote do after he stopped going on quests?
options: He told fantastic stories about knights to children.
quiz.explanation: His house would always be full of children to whom he would tell the most fantastic stories about Knights, Squires, and Noble quests.

quiz.question: According to the glossary, what is the meaning of 'hilarious'?
quiz.explanation: The glossary defines 'hilarious' as 'amusing, entertaining'.

quiz.question: What does 'prodigious' mean?
quiz.explanation: The glossary defines 'prodigious' as 'impressively great in extent'.

quiz.question: Which word means 'a man who served his lord as a mounted soldier in armour'?
quiz.explanation: The glossary defines a 'knight' as a mounted soldier in armour.

quiz.question: What is the meaning of 'combat'?
quiz.explanation: The glossary defines 'combat' as fighting between armed forces.

quiz.question: Match the following appropriately: 1. Steed 2. Ogre 3. Spur
quiz.explanation: Steed is a horse that is ridden. Ogre is a monster/giant. Spur is a spiked wheel worn on a rider's heel.

quiz.question: Find the correct homophone pair from the following.
quiz.explanation: Knight and Night have the same pronunciation but different spellings and meanings.

quiz.question: Identify the homonym pair based on the meaning: 'a water source' and 'in good health'.
quiz.explanation: The word 'well' can mean a water source or being in good health.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'incredible'?
quiz.explanation: Incredible is divided as in-cred-i-ble, which makes 4 syllables.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'courageous'?
quiz.explanation: Courageous is divided as cou-ra-geous, which makes 3 syllables.

quiz.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence according to the Language Check Point.
options: George is the smartest and intelligent staff of this company.
George is the smartest and most intelligent staff of this company.
George is the most smartest and intelligent staff of this company.
George is smartest and more intelligent staff of this company.
quiz.explanation: When two adjectives are used, if the first is superlative, the second must also be superlative.

quiz.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence regarding possessive pronouns and superlatives.
options: It is my the best genre of music.
It is the my best genre of music.
It is my best genre of music.
It is mine the best genre of music.
quiz.explanation: If a possessive pronoun precedes the superlative degree, 'the' cannot be used.

quiz.question: Analogy: Windmill : Ogre :: Flock of sheep : _________
quiz.explanation: Don Quixote mistook the windmill for an ogre and the flock of sheep for a prodigious army.

quiz.question:
Assertion (A): Sancho Panza constantly tried to warn Don Quixote about his illusions.
Reason (R): Sancho wanted the island reward immediately and didn't want delays.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.
quiz.explanation: Sancho warned him because he saw reality (it's just a windmill/sheep), not because he was impatient for the reward.

quiz.question: Which continent is described as the coldest in the world in the grammar crossword?
quiz.explanation: The crossword states: Antarctica is the coldest continent in the world.

quiz.question: Situation: A man keeps arguing that the clouds in the sky are actually invading alien ships, despite his friends proving otherwise. Which character does he resemble?
quiz.explanation: Like Don Quixote, this person substitutes reality with his own fantastical imagination.

quiz.question: In the sentence 'She has tears in her eyes as she tears old photos', what are the meanings of 'tears' respectively?
options: Drops of water ; drops of water
quiz.explanation: The first 'tears' refers to crying (drops of water from eyes), and the second 'tears' means ripping.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'adventure'?
quiz.explanation: Adventure is divided as ad-ven-ture (3 syllables).

quiz.question: What material is described as the hardest in the world in the grammar section?
quiz.explanation: The crossword mentions: Diamonds are the hardest material in the world.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: Kayal is more intelligent and smarter than Saral.
Kayal is smarter and more intelligent than Saral.
Kayal is smart and more intelligent than Saral.
Kayal is smarter and intelligent than Saral.
quiz.explanation: When both adjectives are in the same degree, the adjective having 'more' must come after the adjective having 'er'.

quiz.question: What is the meaning of 'helter-skelter' in the context of the frightened animals?
options: In a calm and orderly manner
quiz.explanation: Helter-skelter means running away in a chaotic and panicked manner.

quiz.question: What did Don Quixote ask Sancho to do if he was afraid of the 'ogres'?
options: Go aside and say your prayers
quiz.explanation: Don told him, 'if you are afraid of them, go aside and say your prayers, while I engage them in combat.'

quiz.question: Which of the following is a homophone for 'fair'?
quiz.explanation: Fair and Fare are homophones.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'hilarious'?
quiz.explanation: Hilarious is divided as hi-lar-i-ous (4 syllables).

quiz.question: What does the word 'spur' refer to?
options: A device with a small spike worn on a rider's heel
quiz.explanation: The glossary defines a spur as a device worn on a rider's heel to urge a horse forward.

quiz.question: Match the homophone pair: 1. See 2. Our 3. Write
quiz.explanation: See sounds like Sea, Our sounds like Hour, Write sounds like Right.

quiz.question:
Assertion (A): Don Quixote never went on any more quests after returning to his estate.
Reason (R): He realized his adventures were just his imagination.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.
quiz.explanation: He stopped going on quests precisely because he finally realized it was all just his imagination.

quiz.question: Which country is mentioned as the largest in the world in the grammar section?
quiz.explanation: The crossword states: Russia is the largest country in the world.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'knight'?
quiz.explanation: Knight has only one vowel sound, so it has 1 syllable.

quiz.question: Identify the homonym 'march' in the sentence: 'We have a march past in March.'
options: Both mean walking in a military manner.
First means military walking, second means the month.
First means the month, second means military walking.
quiz.explanation: A 'march past' is a parade (military walking), and 'March' with a capital M is the month.

quiz.question: How many syllables are in the word 'shepherd'?
quiz.explanation: Shepherd is divided as shep-herd (2 syllables).

quiz.question: According to the 'Discuss and Answer' section, Don Quixote is compared to real-world idealists. Who among the following is NOT given as an example of an idealist?
quiz.explanation: The text lists Nelson Mandela, Mahatma Gandhi, and Martin Luther King Jr. as examples of real-world idealists. Abraham Lincoln is not mentioned.
